coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots: Facile synthesis and multifunctional applications for pH sensing, temperature sensing and RNA-selective imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yanhong
Chen, Ping
Yu, Kangkang
Wu, Zhening
Wang, Na
Yu, Xiaoqi
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatoms doped carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention for their superior optical properties, facile preparation and multifunctional applications in environmental detection, cell imaging and gene delivery. Herein, Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized directly from methyl cellulose (MC), l-cysteine (l-cys) and ethylenediamine (EDA), showing excitation-dependent emission properties and relative high quantum yield (QY) of 12.3%. The obtained N,S-CDs are responsive to pH and temperature, suggesting pH- and temperature-dependent fluorescent features. Moreover, N,S-CDs are utilized to image and exhibit selective location inside the nucleus region both in living and fixed cells. Relative mechanisms have been explored by ribonuclease digestion testing and co-staining analysis with commercial nucleic acid dye. N,S-CDs show the specifical distribution in RNA-rich regions and positive response to RNA in cells. In brief, the developed N,S-CDs possess multi-faced application potency in pH sensing, temperature monitoring and RNA-selective imaging in cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped
Methyl cellulose (MC) pH-temperature response
RNA-selective
Imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21877082 ). We also thank the Comprehensive Training Platform of Specialized Laboratory, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University for sample analysis.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21877082). We also thank the Comprehensive Training Platform of Specialized Laboratory, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University for sample analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of N,S-CDs. (a) TEM image of N,S-CDs. Inset in (a): size distribution histogram of N,S-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs. Inset in (b): images of N,S-CDs solution under white light and UV lamp. (c) Fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs at the different excitation wavelengths. (d) FTIR spectrum. (e) XPS spectrum of N,S-CDs and high-resolution spectrum of C1s (f), N1s (g), O1s (h) and S2p (i).

a) Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs at different temperatures from 10 °C to 70 °C. Inset in a): a linear relationship between FL intensity and temperature. b) The reversible temperature-response of FL intensity from 10 °C to 70 °C. c) The effect of pH on FL intensity of N,S-CDs. d) FL spectra of N,S-CD at different concentrations of NaCl.
Cells imaging of the N,S-CDs in HepG2 cells. (A) The imaging of CLSM. (B) Quantitative analysis of FL intensity in HepG2 cells (λex = 405 nm, λem = 410–500 nm, scale bar = 10 μm).

(A) Cells imaging of the N,S-CDs and NucRed with or without treatment by DNase and RNase. (λex = 405 nm, λem = 410–500 nm, λex = 633 nm, λem = 640–700 nm); (B) Cells imaging of co-staining with N,S-CDs and commercial SYTO RNASelec dye (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–600 nm, scale bar = 10 μm).
Illustration of the synthetic procedure and multifunctional application.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots: Facile synthesis and multifunctional applications for pH sensing, temperature sensing and RNA-selective imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatoms doped carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention for their superior optical properties, facile preparation and multifunctional applications in environmental detection, cell imaging and gene delivery. Herein, Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized directly from methyl cellulose (MC), l-cysteine (l-cys) and ethylenediamine (EDA), showing excitation-dependent emission properties and relative high quantum yield (QY) of 12.3%. The obtained N,S-CDs are responsive to pH and temperature, suggesting pH- and temperature-dependent fluorescent features. Moreover, N,S-CDs are utilized to image and exhibit selective location inside the nucleus region both in living and fixed cells. Relative mechanisms have been explored by ribonuclease digestion testing and co-staining analysis with commercial nucleic acid dye. N,S-CDs show the specifical distribution in RNA-rich regions and positive response to RNA in cells. In brief, the developed N,S-CDs possess multi-faced application potency in pH sensing, temperature monitoring and RNA-selective imaging in cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Ribonucleic acid (RNA), distributed in the cytoplasm and cell nucleus, is the key biomolecule in organisms. RNA participates in the important processes of cell growth such as genetic information transmission, genetic translation, protein synthesis instruction, ribosome formation, and biochemical reactions catalysis [1–3]. Selective labeling and high-resolution visualization of RNA is an attractive field to explore the change of RNA in spatial and temporal distribution under physiological and pathological conditions. Several kinds of methods have been reported for RNA detection in cells, such as fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), oligonucleotide probes recognition, and organic molecular staining [4–6]. However, most of the above methods are complicated and time-consuming. Besides, they usually have a complicated synthesis route and limited application in living cells due to their low permeability, water insolubility and potential cytotoxity [7–9]. Among these, a novel RNA labeling approach based on fluorescent nanomaterials, especially carbon dots, has attracted wide attention because of their eco-friendly preparation, excellent optical properties, great permeability and good biocompatibility in the biologic system.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new series of carbonaceous fluorescent nanomaterials, first identified from the purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [10]. Owing to their small sizes, large surface area, high photostability and low cytotoxicity, CDs have been widely applied in sensing, bioimaging, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, gene and drug delivery [11–18]. Various raw materials, such as fruits, vegetables, flowers, food residues and biomass have been used in CDs preparation [19–21]. The obtained CDs show stable FL performance, but there are still challenges such as low quantum yield (QY), weak membrane permeability and non-targeting distribution in bio-imaging, resulting in limited application in the complicated biologic system. Recently, researchers found that heteroatoms, doped in CDs, can change the inherent properties of materials via adjusting the electronic characteristics and energy gap [22]. Hence, many elements (e.g. N, S, P, B, F) have been introduced in CDs to improve their QY and practical applications [23]. For instance, Chandra et al. synthesized N,S co-doping carbon dots by incorporating nitrogen and sulfur into the CDs. The obtained CDs gave a high QY of 17.59%, showed the ability to sense tiopronin and Hg2+ ions, and cell imaging [24]. Gu et al. developed a N, P co-doped carbon dots (CDs) from Lily bulbs with QY of 17.6%. The prepared CDs exhibited multi-faced potential in heavy metal ions sensing and cell imaging [25]. In our group, we reported a series of heteroatom-doped carbon dots via introducing N and Gadolinium elements into carbon dots. The N-doped CDs prepared by Pcyclen and Ptaea are multifunctional, applied in gene transfection, imaging, and tracking the process of gene delivery [26]. As-prepared Gadolinium-doped CDs were used as a multi-modal (magnetic resonance/fluorescence, MR/FL) agent for gene delivery, cell imaging and tumor diagnosis in vivo [27]. Even so, the application of heteroatom-doped CDs in RNA-selective response and imaging is still rarely reported [28]. In this work, N,S co-doped CDs are synthesized from a biodegradable polymer Methyl Cellulose (MC) which possesses low toxicity and non-immunogenicity [29–32]. The obtained N,S co-doped CDs could sensitively respond to pH and temperature, exhibited application potency as an environmental monitor agent in industrial processes. Interestingly, N,S-CDs showed excellent membrane permeability, intrinsic nucleus location performance and RNA-targeting imaging capacity.

Herein, N,S-co-doped CDs was synthesized using polysaccharides derivative MC, l-cys and EDA as precursor via a one-step hydrothermal method. The N,S-CDs showed good water-solubility, relative high QY and excitation-dependent emission properties. Moreover, the N,S-CDs were responsive to pH and showed FL emission enhancement in the strong alkaline environment. The obtained N,S-CDs exhibited temperature-dependent fluorescent properties. There was a linear correlation between temperature and FL from 10 °C to 70 °C. Besides, the N,S-CDs could locate inside the nucleus and show RNA-selective imaging in living and fixed cells (Scheme 1 ). These results indicated N,S-CDs could be utilized as multifaceted candidates to dynamically monitor the pH and temperature, and to selectively recognize RNA in environmental and biomedical detection.

Methyl Cellulose (MC) was purchased from MP Biomedicals Co., Ltd. (USA). l-cysteine (l-cys) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). MTS (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) were purchased from Promega Corporation (Madison, WI, USA). The Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM), 1640 Medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), Deoxyribonuclease I (DNase I), NucRed Live 647 Ready probes, and SYTO RNASelec green fluorescent cell stain reagent were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc (USA). Calf thymus DNA was purchased from Sigma (USA). Ribonuclease A (RNase A) was obtained from BeyotimeBiotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Doubly distilled water (ddH2O) was used in all of the experiments.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a Hitachi PharmaSpec UV-1900UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi Inspire the Next, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescent spectra were recorded using a Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer (Hitachi Inspire the Next, Tokyo, Japan). The morphology of N,S-CDs was analyzed by Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscopy (FEI, America). Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on Nicolet NEXUS 670FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, America). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, America). Cytotoxicity assay was performed on SPARK Automatic Microplate Reader (TECAN, Austria). Gel electrophoresis results were analyzed by c600 bioanalytical imaging system (Azure, America). Cells were cultured in the CO2 incubator (Thermo Scientific, America). High-resolution cell images were captured using a confocal laser fluorescence microscope (LSM 780, Zeiss, Germany). Relative quantum yields were measured on a Hamamatsu quantum yield spectrometer C11347 Quantaurus QY (Hamamatsu, Japan).

The N,S-CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment. Typically, 1 g MC, 1 g l-cys and 0.8 mL EDA were dissolved in 10 mL ddH2O and stirred drastically by vortex generator. The homogenous mixture was heated at 180 °C for 12 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After that, the brown suspension was obtained. Then, the suspension was collected and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large precipitates. The supernatant was dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500). The solution was lyophilized and stored at 4 °C.

According to an established procedure, the QY of N, S-CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as the standard. Quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 and measured fluorescent emission intensity at 360 nm. The QY of the N, S-CDs was calculated by the following equation:
Q =
Q S
×
I
I S
×
A S
A
×
η
2
η
S
2
where Q represents the QY, I is the total fluorescent emission intensity, A refers to the optical density and η is the refractive index (both of which are 1.33). The standard is the index “S” in the equation. The absorption cuvette was controlled below 0.05 at 360 nm for minimizing the effects of reabsorption.

The pH stability of N,S-CDs was detected in a series of pH buffer solutions. 0.01 M phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) was prepared and adjusted pH by HCl and NaOH solution using pH meter. Meanwhile, the N,S-CDs was dissolved in ddH2O at the concentration of 50 mg/mL, and then 10 μL N,S-CDs suspension was added into 1 mL PBS buffer with a final concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at 330 nm.

The solution of N,S-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) was divided into the EP tubes (1 mL/tube) and incubated at different temperatures for 5 min in heating/cooling dry bath (Thermo Scientific, America) before measuring the fluorescence spectra. To investigate the reversibility, the incubation temperature was adjusted between 10 °C and 70 °C. The relative fluorescence ratio (F/F0) was calculated, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs in the detection temperature and room temperature, respectively.

To assess the intracellular distribution of N,S-CDs, the cells were visualized using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). HepG2 and B16 cells were seeded on 35 mm glass-bottom dishes, respectively. After 24 h culture, the cells were treated with N,S-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) for 1 h. Then the medium was replaced, and cells were co-stained with the commercial probe for 0.5 h at 37 °C. After washing thrice with PBS, the images were captured.

HepG2 cells were fixed with pre-cooled 99% methanol for 1 min. After washing with PBS, the cell membrane was permeabilized by adding 1% Triton X-100 for 2 min and then washed twice with PBS. 70 U/mL DNase or 150 U/mL RNase A was added into the dishes, individually. The cells were incubated at 37 °C for 2 h and washed three times. Then, the pre-treated cells were incubated with N, S-CDs at the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL for 1 h. Subsequently, the cell nucleus was stained by NucRed Live 647 at room temperature for 10 min and washed with PBS three times. Under the CLSM, the cells were imaged.

The N,S co-doped CDs were prepared via a one-pot approach. The morphology and size of N,S-CDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, N,S-CDs showed the spherical shape and narrow size dispersion in water. The diameter of N,S-CDs, with an average of 19 nm, varies from 12 nm to 22 nm, larger than most of CDs. Similar results had been reported in other heteroatoms-doped CDs. Cen et al. synthesized the carbon dots incorporating with N and S elements. The CDs gave a diameter in the range of 10–20 nm [33]. Dang’s group developed a N,S co-doped carbon dots with an average size of 13 nm [24]. The formation of a larger sphere size might relate to the heteroatoms doping. In the synthesis process of N,S-CDs, nitrogen and sulfur atoms were trapped into the framework of CDs and interact with other atoms on the surface which affects the size of CDs [34].

The optical properties of N,S-CDs were characterized by UV–vis absorption and fluorescent (FL) spectrum. The UV–vis spectrum showed a strong peak at 335 nm (Fig. 1b), which is attributed to the CO bands caused by n-π* transition [35]. To investigate the fluorescent feature, the N,S-CDs was directly exposed under the UV lamp at 360 nm. The aqueous solution of N,S-CDs (0.5 mg/ml) was excited and showed a strong blue fluorescent signal. Also, the FL spectrum of N,S-CDs were recorded in Fig. 1b. The maximum emission wavelength was 370 nm while excited at 330 nm. The detailed FL performance was evaluated under the different excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 380 nm. As seen in Fig. 1c, the maximum emission wavelengths shifted from 360 nm to 460 nm, suggesting excitation-dependent emission properties [36]. These features were related to the different particles size, complex surface excited states and different distribution of emission sites on each particle of N,S-CDs [37]. Besides, the QY of N,S-CDs was measured as 12.3%, which was relatively higher than most single atom CDs. Nowadays, more attention was drawn to improve the optical property by doping heteroatoms into CDs [38,39] which was verified to be an effective method to regulate QY via changing the surface state of CDs [40]. Shen et al. revealed that doping N, S elements existed on the surface of CDs with CN and CS bands which could improve the QY via affecting the surface trap and electron system [41]. In our work, N and S elements were verified on the surface as the form of CN contributing to the improvement of QY.

The chemical composition of N,S-CDs were detected by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR). As revealed in Fig. 1d, the broadband corresponds to stretching vibrations of O–H, N–H, and C–H in the range of 2800–3440 cm−1 [42]. The peaks located at 1627 cm−1, 1587 cm−1, 1400 cm−1, and 1036 cm−1 were identified as CN, CC, C–N, and C–O bands, respectively [43–45]. The weak peak at 617 cm−1 is assigned to C–S bonds [41]. Such results verified that N and S elements were successfully doped in the framework of N,S-CDs. To further confirm the elemental composition and surface functional groups of the N,S-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra was measured. Full scan spectra (Fig. 1e) displayed three main peaks at 285 eV (C 1s), 398.5 eV (N 1s) and 531 eV (O 1s) and two weak peaks at 164 eV (S 2p) and 227 eV (S 2s). In detail, the high-resolution spectrum of C1s showed four peaks with binding energy at 284.4, 285.3, 286.0, and 287.9 eV (Fig. 1f). These peaks were attributed to C–C, C–S, C–N/C–S, CN/CO bonds [38]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) divided into three peaks at 398.4, 399.5, and 401.2 eV, related to the N atom in the graphitic-N structure (N–C3), pyridine-like N and N–H bonds, respectively [46]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) exhibited two peaks at 529.8 and 532.5 eV, linked to C–OH and C–O [47]. For the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 1i), the peaks at 162.6, 164.3, 167.1 and 168.5 eV pointed to four forms of S element including C-S (S2p, 3/2), C-S (S2p, 1/2), sulfoxides-S and sulfate-S [48]. These results were in accordance with that of the FTIR spectrum, implied the presence of N and S elements in N,S-CDs surface structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of temperature, pH and salt
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of temperature on fluorescence properties of N,S-CDs, FL spectrum was recorded after N,S-CDs solution incubating at different temperature from 10 °C to 70 °C. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the FL intensity gradually reduced as the temperature increase and showed a linear correlation (R2 = 0.9924) with temperature. The detailed characteristic of temperature response was confirmed via temperature change between 10 °C to 70 °C. The relative fluorescence ratio F/F0 values remained stability following the temperature change from detection temperature to 10 °C, suggesting excellent temperature reversibility of N,S-CDs (Fig. 2b). These results were related to the surface defect of N,S-CDs [49,50]. At higher temperatures, most of the excited electrons backed to the ground state through a non-radiative manner, the proportion of non-radiative channels of the surface (trap/defect) was increased, causing the FL intensity to decrease [51]. The temperature-dependent response property of N,S-CDs opened an avenue for the applications of N,S-CDs in environment temperature monitoring.

The effect of pH on the FL of N,S-CDs was evaluated at different pH values. Fig. 2c showed the FL spectrum in pH from 2 to 13. The FL intensity was steady in pH values between 2 and 6. As the pH increased from 6 to 9, the FL intensity was slightly raised. The FL intensity significantly increased following the pH changed from 9 to 12. To further investigate the pH-responsive feature of N,S-CDs, the FL spectrum was recorded in the pH range of 9–12. The FL intensity and pH values exhibited a linear relationship (R2 = 0.9974) from 10 to 11.5. The pH response property of N,S-CDs was related to the protonation /deprotonation and charge transfer between different types of functional groups on the N,S-CDs surface [36]. Based on the excellent pH-sensitivity performance, the N,S-CDs could be used as a pH indicator in biological and industrial applications.

The salt effect on the FL property of N,S-CDs was also studied. As shown in Fig. 2d, the FL spectrum of N,S-CDs was extremely stable at the different concentrations of NaCl which suggested that the N,S-CDs could maintain stability in a salty environment. Moreover, the photostability of N,S-CDs was also evaluated under a UV lamp. The N,S-CDs solution was directly irradiated under strong UV irradiation for 10 min, 30 min, 1 h, 12 h and 24 h, respectively. The FL intensity of N,S-CDs remained stable even after continuous exposure for 24 h (Fig. S1), indicating the obtained N,S-CDs possessed great photostability.

The cytotoxicity of N,S-CDs was investigated in HepG2 and B16 cells using MTS assay. As shown in Fig. S2, above 80% cell viability was maintained after treatment with N,S-CDs at the concentration from 31.25 to 125 μg/mL for 24 h in HepG2 and B16 cells. At a higher concentration of 1 mg/mL, the cells remained 71.3% cell viability in HepG2 cells, demonstrating the low cytotoxicity of N,S-CDs and it is suitable for living cells imaging.

To investigate the potency of N,S-CDs in living cell imaging, HepG2 and B16 cells were incubated with N,S-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) for 1 h at 37 °C and visualized under CLSM (Fig. S3). The cells showed a bright blue fluorescence signal at the channel 405 nm, which distributed not only in the cytoplasm but also within the nuclear region as densest spots. Similar intracellular location of N,S-CDs was also found in fixed HepG2 cells. By a quantitative analysis of FL profile in cells, it found that FL intensity of spots remarkably enhanced than other regions of the nucleus (Fig. 3 ). Subsequently, The selective location in the nucleus was further confirmed by co-staining with N,S-CDs and commercial nucleus probe NucRed 647. The signal of blue spots showed well overlay with red-labeling nucleus regions both in HepG2 and B16 cells (Fig. S4). All these results suggested that N,S-CDs possesses intrinsic nucleus location capability.

Nucleus-selective staining is an important method for dynamic monitoring changes of nucleic acid in the cell process. Two possible mechanisms for nucleus staining have been reported so far. One is the accumulation of probes in nucleus regions. The other is the interaction with nucleic acid molecules in the cell nucleus [11]. To investigate the reasonable mechanism of N,S-CDs selective-location in the nucleus, Nuclease digestion tests were executed in cells [52]. A commercial DNA probe of NucRed 647 was used as a standard nuclear dye. Firstly, pre-permeabilized HepG2 cells were incubated with DNase for 2 h and stained by N,S-CDs. Then the cells were imaged by CLSM. After digestion by DNase, the fluorescence signal completely disappeared in NucRed 647 group due to DNA degradation. In N,S-CDs staining group, the FL intensity maintained almost the same as the control group (Fig. 4 A). After RNase digestion, nuclear staining of NucRed 647 still remained steady. Nevertheless, FL intensity significantly decrease in N,S-CDs group and no obvious spots were observed in the nucleus region (Fig. 4A). These results indicated that the mechanism of N,S-CDs selective-location in the nucleus was related to specifically recognize RNA in cells. Subsequently, the gel electrophoresis experiment was carried out for analyzing the affinity of N,S-CDs with RNA and DNA molecules. Commercial dye GelRed was used as a control. As presented in Fig. S5, bright bands of RNA were visualized after treated with N,S-CDs, but DNA pre-incubated with N,S-CDs hadn’t been displayed in the gel. Electrophoresis analysis confirmed that N,S-CDs had a higher binding affinity to RNA molecules than DNA, which was in agreement with nuclease digestion tests in cells. To further determine the property of specific response to RNA, a co-staining experiment with commercial RNA-specific tracker (SYTO RNASelec dye) was employed. N,S-CDs exhibited good RNA-specific labeling capacity with Pearson’s coefficient values of 0.84 (Fig. 4B). All these results illustrated N,S-CDs could selectively locate the RNA-rich regions via specific recognition with RNA, providing potential to track RNA molecules in cell growth process research and disease diagnosis.

In summary, a multifunctional N,S-CDs was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal approach using MC, L-cys and EDA as precursors. The as-prepared N,S-CDs showed excellent optical stability, good water solubility, and low cytotoxicity. Meanwhile, the N,S-CDs exhibited attractive properties for pH sensing in alkaline solution, and displayed good temperature-dependent fluorescence response capability and reversibility from 10 °C to 70 °C. More importantly, the N,S-CDs could realize RNA visualization in the biological system by interacting with RNA and locating the RNA-rich regions. Briefly, this work provided a new strategy for green and low-cost synthesis of N,S co-doped CDs with multi-faced potency for sensing and RNA-selective imaging which gave potential application in biologic detection and biomedical diagnosis.

Yanhong Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing, Validation. Ping Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Validation. Kangkang Yu: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Conceptualization. Zhening Wu: Writing - review & editing. Na Wang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Visualization. Xiaoqi Yu: Funding acquisition, Validation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Conceptualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21877082). We also thank the Comprehensive Training Platform of Specialized Laboratory, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University for sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106248.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Chow, Advancing small ligands targeting RNA for better binding affinity and specificity: A study of structural influence through molecular design approach, Sensor Actuat B- Chem. 262 (2018) 386-394.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent indicator displacement assays to identify and characterize small molecule interactions with RNA
